coredata.dc:title: Time-dependent phosphorescence from carbon dots enables multidimensional photoactivated printing and tunable molecular calculations
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Jianwen
Tang, Zhaorun
Yin, Junping
Guan, Zhihao
Wei, Ruyi
Ke, Xianwen
Liu, Xinghai
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials exhibit fascinating optical properties with great potential for various applications in the fields of luminescent displays and information encryption. However, most afterglow materials rely on pre-processing techniques such as molding and inkjet printing, greatly limiting the portability of their applications. In this study, we propose a reversible photoactivated phosphorescent anti-counterfeiting material. A dynamic photo-printable afterglow film that can be naturally erased was developed by doping carbon dots (CDs) of ofloxacin into polylactic acid (PLA). The material exhibits a 15 s yellow-to-green dynamic afterglow, while the lifetime of the material jumps from 2.5 ms to a maximum of 625 ms under continuous UV irradiation for less than 60 s. Characterization results showed that dynamic RTP originated from external oxygen-containing functional groups and internal nitrogen heterocycles with different decay rates within CDs. Further studies suggested that photoactivation properties should be attributed to the highly oxygen permeable but UV responsive structure of PLA. While oxygen in the membrane was excited by UV light, cross-linking occurs between PLA molecules, providing a rigid environment for CDs and limiting the subsequent entry of oxygen, further extending its lifetime. Based on the above advantages, this dynamic afterglow material has been successfully applied in light-emitting displays and optical molecular logic operation unit design.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Ofloxacin
Carbon dots
Polylactic acid
Dynamic afterglow
Photoactivation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microstructures of the CDs and CDs-PLA films
Dynamic RTP Features of CDs and CDs-PLA during irradiation
Programmable molecular Operator Units and photo-printing

Financial support was provided by National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2023YFE0105500) and Wuhan University postgraduate research credit course project of Intelligent Packaging and Food Safety (Grant No. 1506/413100017). The authors also thanked the Core Facility of Wuhan University for FTIR, TEM, SEM, XPS tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The preparation process of CDs-PLA film. b) Schematic diagram of the photoactive printing process. c) Phosphorescence photos of CDs-PLA film after photoactive printing.

a) The tem images (20 nm) of CDs. b) The SEM images (10 μm) of CDs-PLA. c) The FTIR spectra of CDs, PLA, and CDs-PLA. d) The XRD patterns of CDs, PLA, and CDs-PLA. High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of e) CDs, f) CDs-PLA.

a) The UV–Vis spectra of ofloxacin, CDs, PLA, and CDs-PLA. b) Excitation-emission fluorescence mapping of CDs. c) The phosphorescence spectra of CDs-PLA for different irradiation time (0–60 s). d) Normalized time-dependent phosphorescence spectra of CDs-PLA. e) CIE coordinates of phosphorescence spectra of CDs-PLA. f) The phosphorescence spectra of CDs-PLA for different irradiation time (70–900 s). g) Phosphorescence lifetime histogram of CDs-PLA for different irradiation time. h) Phosphorescence lifetime curves of CDs-PLA for different excitation wavelength.

a) Schematic diagram of the photoactivation process of CDs-PLA. b) Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) signals of CDs-PLA. c) The FTIR spectrum of CDs-PLA for different irradiation time. d) Phosphorescence lifetime changes of CDs-PLA film during the excitation and erasing cycles.

a) Logic gate circuit of half adder and half subtractor. b) The photoactivation of CDs-PLA by different power UV light (318 mW/cm2 and 212 mW/cm2). c) The photoactivation of CDs-PLA by different power UV light (635 mW/cm2 and 212 mW/cm2). d) The truth table for half adder. e) The truth table for half subtractor. f) Schematic of multi-layer photo-printing encryption of materials.

head.ce:title: Time-dependent phosphorescence from carbon dots enables multidimensional photoactivated printing and tunable molecular calculations

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Engineering Technology Research Center of Spectrum and Imaging Instrument

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials exhibit fascinating optical properties with great potential for various applications in the fields of luminescent displays and information encryption. However, most afterglow materials rely on pre-processing techniques such as molding and inkjet printing, greatly limiting the portability of their applications. In this study, we propose a reversible photoactivated phosphorescent anti-counterfeiting material. A dynamic photo-printable afterglow film that can be naturally erased was developed by doping carbon dots (CDs) of ofloxacin into polylactic acid (PLA). The material exhibits a 15 s yellow-to-green dynamic afterglow, while the lifetime of the material jumps from 2.5 ms to a maximum of 625 ms under continuous UV irradiation for less than 60 s. Characterization results showed that dynamic RTP originated from external oxygen-containing functional groups and internal nitrogen heterocycles with different decay rates within CDs. Further studies suggested that photoactivation properties should be attributed to the highly oxygen permeable but UV responsive structure of PLA. While oxygen in the membrane was excited by UV light, cross-linking occurs between PLA molecules, providing a rigid environment for CDs and limiting the subsequent entry of oxygen, further extending its lifetime. Based on the above advantages, this dynamic afterglow material has been successfully applied in light-emitting displays and optical molecular logic operation unit design.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The characteristics of photo-stimulation include convenient and straightforward accessibility, powerful remote manipulation capabilities, and rapid modulation of response [1]. Introduction of a specific unit can render the structure of a material light-responsive, thereby conferring it with distinct properties and applications, leveraging the inherent advantages of photo-stimulation. Numerous advancements have been made in technologies pertaining to optoelectronic devices, molecular logic gates, as well as data storage and recording [2–5]. However, the current research primarily focuses on light-induced unidirectional changes in the physicochemical properties of materials, with comparatively less emphasis on investigating their impact on luminescent properties. Conversely, the field of long afterglow materials have garnered significant attention in a number of fields, such as luminescent displays [6], information encryption [7], and bio-imaging [8]. The unique optical properties of these materials are responsible for this phenomenon. Based on current research, it can be inferred that ultraviolet irradiation is a fundamental prerequisite for the manifestation of long afterglow emission in materials, which aligns with the requirements of photoactivatable printing technology. The fabrication of afterglow materials typically necessitates pre-treatments such as molding [9] and printing [10,11] to achieve the desired display effect. However, these pre-treatments significantly limit the range of applications for these materials. Consequently, the advancement of display anti-counterfeiting in the field necessitates the development of long afterglow materials that possess stable and adjustable display effects.

The phenomenon of long afterglow can be comprehended as the facilitation of triple-state exciton generation in the chromophore and the suppression of triple-state exciton quenching [12–14]. This can be accomplished through various mechanisms, including small molecule crystallization [15], heavy atom effect [16], host–guest assembly [17], and others [18–20]. In recent years, numerous techniques have been employed in the synthesis of printable afterglow materials [21–23]. Upon prolonged exposure to ultraviolet light, the structure of these materials undergoes a structural transformation. The non-radiative vibration of guests is effectively suppressed due to the rigid substrate structure and the presence of covalent bonding between the guest and host, or intermolecular forces. The suppression effectively achieves room temperature phosphorescent RTP emission. The crystals PyCz-B and PyCz-N, cultivated by Huang et al. [24], exhibited a yellow afterglow after being irradiated for 1 and 6 min, respectively. However, single color and non-flexibility limit the potential applications of crystals. The phosphorescent polymers with strong bonding abilities were synthesized by Huang et al [25] using three random copolymers with different polymer compositions as substrates. Upon exposure to 365 nm UV light, the polymer exhibited phosphorescence, with a maximum lifetime exceeding 5 s observed after 1 min of irradiation. Bi et al [26]. incorporated B-CDs into the amorphous polymer, resulting in a remarkable enhancement of the lifetime from 2.53 ms to 720 ms after 4 min of irradiation. Moreover, the afterglow can be easily erased through heating, showcasing its potential for erasable and photo-printable afterglow. Nevertheless, the majority of the previous studies require an extended duration of UV irradiation (>60 s) in order to deplete triplet oxygen before initiating the long-lasting afterglow [27–29]. The lack of fast, dynamic photoactivatable phosphorescent moieties hinders the capture of vibrant colors in photoactivatable materials, posing a significant challenge for the development of long-lived and dynamically glowing afterglow prints.

Here, as shown in Fig. 1 a, Complexes of CDs and PLA (CDs-PLA) with dynamic afterglow were prepared using a two-step method, in which the variables involved, such as hydrothermal time and solvent, were optimized through control experiments (Figure S1 and S2). Ofloxacin was employed as the precursor in the first step. CDs can be conveniently synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal approach. In the second step, PLA was utilized as the primary matrix. By blending and stirring CDs with PLA dissolved in N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), a significant number of hydrogen bonds were formed to securely immobilize the CDs, thereby inhibiting non-radiative transitions of chromophores and achieving an ultra-long-lived afterglow effect. Surprisingly, due to the diverse functional groups and conjugated structures present both inside and outside the CDs, the films exhibited excellent excitation-dependent behavior with dynamic photoactivated afterglow (Fig. 1b). Following a brief activation process (<60 s), the material's lifetime increased from 2.5 ms to 625 ms, demonstrating a dynamic afterglow accompanied by a phosphorescence quantum yield (PHQY) of 12.8 %. This phenomenon can be attributed to the unique properties of both CDs and PLA films. The removal of oxygen from the film under 365 nm UV irradiation resulted in the excitation of different emission centers within the CDs, leading to a composite material that exhibited a time-dependent room-temperature phosphorescence (TDRTP), ranging from yellow to green (Fig. 1c). Compared to previous researches, CDs-PLA lifetimes were significantly improved, while the activation time was greatly reduced and the photoluminescence properties became more diverse. Moreover, the incorporation of PLA matrix endowed the CDs composites with excellent degradability[30] and environmental stability, thereby expanding their potential for green applications in multidimensional encryption and information storage.

The synthesis of CDs was achieved through a one-step hydrothermal reaction. A solution containing 50 mg of Ofloxacin in 20 mL of deionized water was homogeneously mixed and then transferred to a hydrothermal reactor with a Teflon liner, which was heated at 200℃ for 6 h to obtain the crude product of CDs. After dialysis for 12 h using dialysis bags with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da, the purified CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

A solution was prepared by dissolving 5 mg of CDs powder in 5 ml of deionized water, resulting in a concentration of 1 mg/mL. Subsequently, 1.5 mL of this CDs solution was homogeneously dispersed in 10 mL of DMF along with 300 mg of PLA. The mixture was then heated at 80℃ and stirred for 2 h to obtain a well-mixed solution containing 0.5 % CDs-PLA composite. This solution was poured into a glass container and dried at 80℃ to remove water and DMF, yielding the final CDs-PLA composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructures of the CDs and CDs-PLA films
ce:section.ce:para: The internal morphological characteristics of CDs were examined using TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, CDs exhibit a uniform monodisperse state in aqueous solution, with particle sizes ranging from 4 nm to 8 nm. High-resolution TEM image reveals well-defined lattice stripes within the CDs, exhibiting a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the graphite (100) plane. Meanwhile, the surface structure of CDs-PLA demonstrated in SEM image (Fig. 2b).

The surface composition of CDs was analyzed using FTIR, XRD and XPS. From the obtained FTIR spectra (Fig. 2c), it is evident that CDs possess a similar surface structure to the precursor material (Figure S3). The absorption peak observed at around 1750 cm−1 corresponds to the presence of carbonyl groups on the CD surface, while the absorption band in the range of 1300–1500 cm−1 originates from nitrogen heterocyclic structures embedded within CDs. XRD patterns (Fig. 2d) revealed that, in contrast to ofloxacin (Figure S4), both pristine and doped CDs exhibited an amorphous morphology when incorporated into PLA.

The XPS analysis also provides insights into the surface structure of the CDs and CDs-PLA composites. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2e and 2f) exhibited three peaks at 284.52 eV, 285.80 eV, 287.51 eV and 288.32 eV, attributed to C-C/C = C, C-N/C-O, C = O/ C = N and C(O) = O, and the O1s spectrum (Figure S5) can be fitted to three bands centered at 530.46 eV, 531.79 eV, and 533.35 eV belonging to C = O, O-H/C-O and O-C = O. And N1s spectrum (Figure S6) reveals that N in CDs is mainly present as graphitic N and pyrrolic N.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic RTP Features of CDs and CDs-PLA during irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the emission of CDs, the UV absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous solutions of ofloxacin and CDs were measured (Fig. 3 a). The results indicate that the aqueous solution of ofloxacin exhibits a strong absorption peak at 300–400 nm, which corresponds to the n-π* transition in the molecule, including the nitrogen heterocycles and carbonyl structures[31]. Furthermore, the absorption peaks of CDs are broader than those of the precursors, which indicates the expansion of nitrogen heterocycles during the formation of CDs and the increase of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface due to high temperature and pressure. Both aqueous solutions of CDs and CDs-PLA film exhibit significant excitation-dependent emission based on Fig. 3b and Figure S7, indicating the presence of two emission centers. The stability of the CDs was evaluated under ambient conditions, the emission of the CDs remained above 90 % when the CDs were placed in ambient conditions for 30 days (Figure S8). This evidence demonstrates the excellent stability of the prepared CDs.

As can be seen from Figure S9, influenced by both doping ratio and degree of aggregation[12], when the mass fraction of CDs reaches the optimal doping ratio of 0.5 wt%, the photoactivation of CDs-PLA from almost non-RTP emission to a strong dynamic afterglow after 60 s of irradiation can be clearly observed. Simultaneously, the full activation time was found to be related to the power of the UV light (Figure S10). Therefore, the effect of photoactivation time on phosphorescence emission was investigated with 0.5 wt%, 635 mW/cm2. The possibility of PLA phosphorescence may have also been precluded to ensure that the afterglow comes from CDs (Figure S11). As shown in Fig. 3c, the phosphorescence emission was almost undetectable before irradiation, whereas after 10 s of continuous irradiation, the phosphorescence emission was enhanced, and with continued irradiation, the RTP intensity at 535 nm reached the highest value by 60 s of UV irradiation, and the lifetime was enhanced to 625 ms (Figure S12 and S13). When the UV irradiation time was longer than 60 s, the intensity of the phosphorescence emission began to decrease gradually (Fig. 3f), and the lifetime was slightly reduced, and after the After 900 s of continuous irradiation, both the emission intensity and lifetime hardly decreases any more (Figure S14 and S15). The phenomenon of diminished emission may originate from the process of photobleaching of CDs[32,33], as evidenced by bleaching experiments of CDs fluorescence under continuous irradiation (Figure S16). And thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) has been excluded through phosphorescent spectra at different temperatures (Figure S17).

In addition, the principle of dynamic afterglow was further investigated using time-resolved phosphorescence spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 3d and S16, the phosphorescence spectra of CDs-PLA exhibit dual emission centers, consistent with the previous studies reported for levofloxacin CDs[34]. These emission centers are observed at 520 nm and 605 nm, corresponding to excitation wavelengths of 310 nm and 425 nm respectively, each center has different excitation and emission spectra (Figure S18 and S19), resulting in green and red emissions. Notably, the two emission centers display distinct decay rates over time. Specifically, the red center exhibits a faster decay rate while the green center shows a slower decay rate, leading to an intriguing transition from orange to yellow to green emissions as demonstrated by the CIE plot (Fig. 3e). Furthermore, we measured the phosphorescence lifetimes of these emission centers (Fig. 3h), revealing that CDs-PLA possesses a prolonged phosphorescence lifetime exceeding 566 ms at an emission wavelength of 520 nm and an additional lifetime of 347 ms at an emission wavelength of 605 nm. Moreover, the quantum yield for phosphorescence is determined to be approximately 12.8 %. Overall, the phosphorescence performance of CDs-PLA under continuous irradiation showed a rapid increase in the first 60 s, followed by a slight decrease and finally stabilized, as shown in Fig. 3g.

Previous studies have demonstrated that systems based on polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), among other materials, typically exhibit stable emission[35]. In contrast, polymer systems doped with PMMA[36] and epoxy resins[37] generally demonstrate photo-activated RTP functionality involving oxygen quenching. The high permeability of these polymer matrices to oxygen and their limited ability to effectively block oxygen from reaching the chromophore contribute to this phenomenon. In PLA, this theory can also be confirmed by the spectra obtained under nitrogen and oxygen environments. Although the spectral differences after activation are not significant, more exposure is required for activation in oxygen compared to nitrogen and air. (Figure S20-S22). Under UV light, the triplet oxygen is scavenged and the materials realize phosphorescence emission enhanced with irradiation duration (Fig. 4 a). Herein, a pronounced electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) signal for singlet oxygen was also detected in these doped CDs-PLA films (Fig. 4b). However, our experiments revealed that the initial photoactivation process took significantly longer than subsequent activation time. During subsequent photoactivation, the phosphorescence intensity of the CDs-PLA film reached its maximum value in approximately 20 s of irradiation, which is considerably shorter than the initial photoactivation process. This suggests that the initial photoactivation process is not solely responsible for converting triplet oxygen to singlet oxygen. Cross linking reaction was confirmed by FTIR spectra of CDs-PLA acquired during photoactivation. As depicted in Fig. 4c, a significant decrease in hydroxyl groups (O-H) at 3100–3600 cm−1 was observed, indicating a cross-linking reaction within the film. The carbonyl peak (CO) showed a slight increase at 1730 cm−1, suggesting the generation of free radicals and cross-linking between PLA components. The XPS spectra also reflect the same changes in hydroxyl and carbonyl groups (Figure S23). The FTIR profiles of CDs, PLA, CDs-PVA and CDs-PVP membranes before and after irradiation (Figure S24-S27) demonstrated that UV irradiation had minimal impact on the membrane structure when PVA and PVP were doped with CDs. In contrast, PLA exhibited similar changes to CDs-PLA at both 3100–3600 cm−1 and 1730 cm−1, indicating that the cross linking originated from the interaction between CDs and PLA. The reduction of hydroxyl groups following CD irradiation is consistent with the observed photobleaching phenomenon in CDs. Furthermore, it was observed that when oxygen-permeable PMMA matrix was doped with CDs, CD-PMMA exhibited photoactivation properties. However, its activation duration was considerably shorter than that of CDs-PLA. FTIR spectrum (Figure S28) demonstrated no significant alterations in functional groups for CD-PMMA following irradiation. Meanwhile, considering that PLA has different crystalline forms, the XRD spectra (Figure S29) before and after irradiation were used to exclude their influence. Therefore, it can be hypothesized that the superior photoactivation properties observed in PLA matrix may be attributed to cross-linking reaction. To demonstrate the universality of the method, Hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) with carboxyl or hydroxyl groups was chosen as the material matrix. Figure S30 and S31 presents the phosphorescent activation spectrum of CDs-HEC and a comparison graph of the maximum intensity between CDs-HEC and CDs-CMC, respectively. Due to differences in permeability, CDs-HEC requires a longer activation time, while CDs-CMC did not exhibit photoactivation. However, the maximum emission intensity of both materials is nearly identical, demonstrating the positive effect of UV irradiation on RTP.

In order to assess the applicability of CDs-PLA material, its stability during multiple irradiations was investigated as shown in Fig. 4d. After subjecting the material to 10 cycles of excitation-erasing process (initial activation 60 s, subsequent activation 20 s), a slight reduction in material lifetime was observed. Additionally, spectrograms and afterglow decay curves of CDs-PLAs stored under high temperature, high humidity conditions for 7 days and ambient condition for 30 days were analyzed (Figure S31-S34), confirming the film's adaptability to environmental factors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Programmable molecular Operator Units and photo-printing
ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the utilization of luminescent properties in RTP materials for various information processing and encryption functions has become particularly challenging[38–40]. Considering the advantage of long-lived emission in eliminating background fluorescence interference, researchers have attempted to employ RTP materials for constructing optical logic gates[40–42]. Based on the aforementioned analysis, it can be observed that the dynamic RTP properties of CDs-PLA are highly reliant on both the degree of cross-linking and irradiation light source power. Under fixed-power UV light irradiation, CDs-PLA membranes treated with cross-linking exhibit a faster photoactivation rate compared to their initial irradiation. Additionally, higher irradiation power can accelerate both photoactivation and photobleaching processes. Therefore, by reasonably controlling the logic channel, an adjustable optical logic module for half adder and half subtractor (Fig. 5 a) was constructed. Taking half adder as an example, we define the degree of optical crosslinking (1 for cross-linked and 0 for uncross-linked) as first additive number (A), while setting the irradiation power (1 for strong and 0 for weak) as second additive number (B). The relative intensity of phosphorescence is selected as carry (CAR) at 30 s, and represents sum (SUM) at 300 s, respectively. It outputs 1 when the phosphorescence intensity (I) reached 50 % of its maximal intensity (I0, phosphorescence intensity of newly made CDs-PLA films when irradiated for 60 s), otherwise it outputs 0. The intensity of phosphorescence emission from CDs-PLA at 525 nm has been recorded in Fig. 5b. By changing the irradiation power from the original value of 318 mW/cm2 to 635 mW/cm2 (Fig. 5c) and modifying corresponding definitions to include half subtractors for minuend (X), subtrahend (Y), borrowed bits (BOR), and difference output (DIF), I/I0 can be utilized to convert additive outputs into subtractive ones. This enables recognition of combinations involving A, B, CAR, SUM as truth-table-based half (Fig. 5d) adders and X, Y, BOR, DIF as half subtractors (Fig. 5e). To our knowledge, this is the first example demonstrating switchable addition and subtraction calculations using RTP.

The potential of photoactivated material in information encryption was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 5f, the CDs-PLA film underwent a two-step processing procedure. Initially, the upper right corner of the material was exposed to irradiation from a 365 nm UV lamp through a mask for 60 s, resulting in the formation of a cross-linked ‘moon’-shaped area. After waiting 120 s for spontaneous erasing, the ‘geese’ pattern was printed on the lower left side using the same method. When briefly excited, only the dynamic afterglow of the ‘goose’ shape was clearly observable. Approximately 20 s into irradiation, gradual emergence of the moon pattern can also be observed due to its faster activation nature within this crosslinked region. Following an additional 60 s of irradiation, complete activation occurred and all patterned information is erased. This process is reversible and can be utilized reprinting once oxygen has diffused into the material.

In conclusion, we successfully developed a room-temperature phosphorescent film, CDs-PLA, exhibiting time-resolved properties through the doping of CDs with PLA. Under UV irradiation, oxygen was removed from the interior of CDs-PLA, activating the phosphorescence of the CDs and resulting in a dynamic afterglow that shifts from orange to green due to variations in the lifetimes of luminescent centers both inside and outside the CDs. Furthermore, the photoactivation process of CDs-PLA involves photo-crosslinking and photobleaching, which are closely associated with irradiation intensity and the degree of crosslinking. Based on experimental results and characterization tests, we hypothesize that UV irradiation creates a rigid environment for the CDs by exciting singlet oxygen and inducing PLA crosslinking. Given the intriguing optical properties exhibited by CDs-PLA under various conditions, it has been effectively applied to phosphorescence-dependent molecular operations and multidimensional information encryption. This discovery not only enhances our understanding of photoactivatable dynamic RTP materials but also broadens their range of applications.

Jianwen Zeng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhaorun Tang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Junping Yin: Supervision. Zhihao Guan: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ruyi Wei: Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xianwen Ke: Supervision, Conceptualization. Xinghai Liu: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: J.Z. and Z.T. contributed equally to this work. Financial support was provided by National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2023YFE0105500) and Wuhan University postgraduate research credit course project of Intelligent Packaging and Food Safety (Grant No. 1506/413100017). The authors also thanked the Core Facility of Wuhan University for FTIR, TEM, SEM, XPS tests.
Thanks to eceshi (www.eceshi.com) for the variable temperature phosphorescence test.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.157819.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Expansion–contraction of photoresponsive artificial muscle regulated by host–guest interactions

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Confining isolated chromophores for highly efficient blue phosphorescence
